coredata.dc:title: A novel molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor based on graphene quantum dots coated on hollow nickel nanospheres with high sensitivity and selectivity for the rapid determination of bisphenol S
coredata.dc:creator: Rao, Hanbing
Zhao, Xun
Liu, Xin
Zhong, Ji
Zhang, Zhaoyi
Zou, Ping
Jiang, Yuanyuan
Wang, Xianxiang
Wang, Yanying
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a novel molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor (MIECS) based on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) coated on hollow nickel nanospheres (hNiNS) for the rapid determination of bisphenol S (BPS) was proposed for the first time. HNiNS and GQDs as electrode modifications were used to enlarge the active area and electron-transport ability for amplifying the sensor signal, while molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) film was electropolymerized by using pyrrole as monomer and BPS as template to detect BPS via cyclic voltammetry (CV). Scanning electron microscope (SEM), energy-dispersive spectrometry (EDS), CV and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were employed to characterize the fabricated sensor. Experimental conditions, such as molar ratio of monomer to template, electropolymerization cycles, pH, incubation time and elution time were optimized. The DPV response of the MIECS to BPS was obtained in the linear range from 0.1 to 50μM with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.03μM (S/N = 3) under the optimized conditions. The MIECS exhibited excellent response towards BPS with high sensitivity, selectivity, good reproducibility, and stability. In addition, the proposed MIECS was also successfully applied for the determination of BPS in the plastic samples with simple sample pretreatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly imprinted polymers
Electrochemical sensor
Hollow nickel nanospheres
Graphene quantum dots
Bisphenol S
Electrochemical determination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface characterization of the different electrodes
Electrochemical characterization of different modified electrodes
The effect of the molar ratio functional monomer to template molecule
The optimal response characteristics of MMIP sensor
Reproducibility and stability of the sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of Ni-Cu alloy layer (A), hNiNS (B), hNiNS/GQDs (C), hNiNS/GQDs/MIPs (D) and hNiNS/GQDs/NIPs (E).

(A) CVs in 0.5M KCl solution containing 5.0mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-, bare GCE (a), hNiNS/GCE (b), hNiNS/GQDs/GCE (c), hNiNS/GQDs/MIPs/GCE after removing the template (d), hNiNS/GQDs/NIPs/GCE (e); (B) DPVs of bare GCE (a), hNiNS/GQDs/GCE (b), hNiNS/GQDs/MIPs/GCE (c), hNiNS/GQDs/NIPs/GCE (d) in 0.1M CBS (pH 4.0) after immersed in 5μM BPS for 90s at optimal conditions.

(A) DPVs of different concentration of BPS (a-k): 0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 10, 20, 30,40 ,50μM on the hNiNS/GQDs/MIPs/GCE in 0.1M CBS (pH 4.0); (B) and (C) relationship between peak current and BPS concentration (n = 3, 95% confidence level).

(A) DPV voltammograms of 25μM BPS and against the equivalent concentration of phenol, hydroquinone, p-nitrophenol, and sulfanilic acid; (B) DPV current responses of hNiNS/GQDs/MIPs/GCE in BPS and the analogues. (Two equivalent concentration levels, 5 and 25μM, were used.).
Schematic illustration of the preparation of hNiNS/GQDs/GCE and electrochemical detection process.
Comparison of other analytical methods for BPS with this proposed MIECS.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: DLLME-UHPLC-MS/MS, dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction ultrahigh-performance liquid chromatography –tandem mass spectrometry; On-line SPE fast LC-MS/MS, on-line solid phase extraction fast liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry; SPME-GC-MS, solid-phase micro extraction gas chromatography-mass spectrometry; GO, graphene oxide.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: The authors wish it to be known that, in their opinions, Hanbing Rao and Xun Zhao should be regarded as joint First Authors.

head.ce:title: A novel molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor based on graphene quantum dots coated on hollow nickel nanospheres with high sensitivity and selectivity for the rapid determination of bisphenol S

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Ya’an 625014, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a novel molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor (MIECS) based on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) coated on hollow nickel nanospheres (hNiNS) for the rapid determination of bisphenol S (BPS) was proposed for the first time. HNiNS and GQDs as electrode modifications were used to enlarge the active area and electron-transport ability for amplifying the sensor signal, while molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) film was electropolymerized by using pyrrole as monomer and BPS as template to detect BPS via cyclic voltammetry (CV). Scanning electron microscope (SEM), energy-dispersive spectrometry (EDS), CV and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were employed to characterize the fabricated sensor. Experimental conditions, such as molar ratio of monomer to template, electropolymerization cycles, pH, incubation time and elution time were optimized. The DPV response of the MIECS to BPS was obtained in the linear range from 0.1 to 50μM with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.03μM (S/N = 3) under the optimized conditions. The MIECS exhibited excellent response towards BPS with high sensitivity, selectivity, good reproducibility, and stability. In addition, the proposed MIECS was also successfully applied for the determination of BPS in the plastic samples with simple sample pretreatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphenol S, [4, 4′– sulfonyldiphenol], has been commonly used as the substitute for bisphenol A to produce polycarbonate plastic with multifarious applications such as optical, electrical, automotive, media, and electronics equipment, packaging, appliances, medical, and construction (Eladak et al., 2015). The replacement of dimethylmethylene (C(CH3)2) group of BPA by the sulfonyl (SO2) group of BPS (Fig. S1) (Boucher et al., 2016) offers several benefits, such as higher rigidity, increased resistance to organic solvents, improved dimensional stability, and better wetting of glass reinforcements (Lotti et al., 2011; Molina-Molina et al., 2013). However, studies show that BPS retains similar estrogenic activity, shows increased environmental persistence (Mathew et al., 2014) and the ability of developing chronic diseases, including obesity, diabetes, atherosclerosis, genital malformation, hepatic disturbances, and even cancers (Helies-Toussaint et al., 2014). Up to now, the methods of detecting BPS mainly depend on chromatographic analysis like LC-ESI-MS (Yang et al., 2014), UHPLC-MS/MS (Vela-Soria et al., 2014), P-GC-MS (Becerra and Odermatt, 2012) and online-SPE-HPLC-MS/MS (Zhou et al., 2014b). Although these techniques detect BPS in the water environment with high accuracy and are based on common analytical methods, they also require complex pretreatment steps, time-consuming analysis, expensive instruments, and well-trained operators (Zhu et al., 2016). Hence, it is of great importance to find a more convenient way to detect BPS with high sensitivity and selectivity.

With high selectivity, excellent robustness, chemical stability and practicability (Kor and Zarei, 2016; Rezaei et al., 2013), the MIPs have been widely developed to recognize some particular target molecules in recent years. MIPs are typically synthesized by polymerizing functional monomers in the presence of a template molecule (Li et al., 2015a). After the template molecules are extracted from the MIPs, the MIPs forms the cavities of the specific molecule so that the MIPs can recognize the target molecule by strong chemisorption (Liu et al., 2017a). MIECS combines molecularly imprinted technique (MIT) and electrochemical sensor. It has attracted much attention and has been widely used to detect different molecules (Rao et al., 2017; Xia et al., 2016). However, the poor conductivity and electrocatalytical activity of the MIECS leads to lower sensitivity. As a result, many efforts have been made to enhance its conductivity by using electrocatalytical nanocomposites (Liang et al., 2017; Rao et al., 2017), conductive functional monomers and functionalized conductive nanocomposites, such as pyrrole (Wang et al., 2014; Yang et al., 2016), and monomeric graphene quantum dots (Prasad et al., 2017).

Different metal nanoparticles/nanowires, such as Au, Pt, Ni nanoparticles/ nanowires (Atar et al., 2016; Beluomini et al., 2017; Lu et al., 2013b; Shervedani et al., 2014) and PtAu, AuAg bimetallic nanoparticles (Cui et al., 2015; Thanh et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2017), have been used to enhance electrocatalytical activity of MIECS. In particular, hNiNS prepared by electrodeposition offer lower price, better electron-transfer rate, better-controlled, and easier in-situ preparation (Li et al., 2015b; Liu et al., 2017b), when compared to Ni nanoparticles (D'Addato et al., 2012) and other noble metal nanoparticles (Cui et al., 2015) that are modified on a flat electrode. For example, Liu et al. (2017b) and Li et al. (2015b) used flaked hollow nickel nanospheres and 3D nanoporous nickel skeleton as an electrode modifier to serve as the loading platform for MIPs to enhance its conductivity, which allowed to detect metronidazole and dopamine with a remarkably low detection limit.

GQDs are 0 D nanomaterial converted by 2 D graphene (Neubeck et al., 2010) under hydrothermal conditions (Wang et al., 2011). GQDs have stable luminescence, chemical stability, suitable conductivity, as well as low toxicity (Tan et al., 2016; Zhu et al., 2012a, 2012b) with diameter less than 10nm (Roushani and Abdi, 2014). Zhao et al. (2011) and Mahmoud Roushani and Roushani and Abdi (2014) have reported that GQDs were used to fabricate the electrochemical sensor based on the strong interaction between single-stranded DNA and GQDs and combine with riboflavin as a novel type of nanocomposite for detecting persulfate, respectively.

Herein, we constructed a novel MIECS based on hNiNS coating GQDs and MIP film for BPS quantitative analysis. As shown in Scheme 1, the GQDs were coated on hNiNS were and used as electrode modifications to increase the active area, improve the conductivity, and enhance the electrochemical response for sensitively detecting BPS. The proposed sensor was constructed via electropolymerization of pyrrole in the presence of BPS template molecules onto the surface of a glassy carbon electrode modified with the hNiNS coating GQDs. After the template molecules were extracted from the MIP film, the senor could detect BPS molecules through DPV based on hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking interactions. The resulting new modified electrode showed high sensitivity, selectivity, good reproducibility, stability, and a wide detection range. Furthermore, the proposed electrochemical sensor was successfully applied to detect BPS in different plastic samples.
All reagents and apparatus required for the experiment, and detailed experimental steps were included in the Supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface characterization of the different electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphologies and element distribution of different electrodes were characterized by SEM and EDS. As shown in Fig. 1 A and Fig. S2A, the Ni-Cu alloy modified electrode is composed from small alloy blocks. According to results from EDS, the atomic ratio of Ni and Cu is close to 1:2, which can be explained by the fact that it is easier to reduce and form particles from Cu2+. After dealloying Cu from the Ni-Cu alloy, the small alloy blocks were transformed into hollow nickel nanospheres (Fig. 1 B), while the ratio of Ni to Cu was changed into 6:1 (Fig. S2B). To achieve GQDs modification, the GQD nanoparticles were coated on a uniform nanocomposite layer formed on the hNiNS/GCE (Fig. 1 C). Fig. 1 D and E respectively show the SEM images of the BPS-imprinted polypyrrole film and non-imprinted polypyrrole film on the hNiNS/GQDs/GCE. It can be seen, that the MIP film has a more porous and rougher surface than the non-imprinted polymer (NIP) film. The formed cavities after elution of organic solvent and electrochemical elution on the polypyrrole film are likely caused by the structure of target molecule, BPS. As a result, MIP film demonstrated better adsorption ability than the NIP film (Rao et al., 2017).

The UV–vis absorbance and photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of the prepared GQDs are shown in Fig. S3. The UV–vis spectrum of GQDs shows a broad absorbance centered at 300nm. The strongest PL adsorption peak is located at 436nm under the excitation wavelength of 320nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of different modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The cyclic voltammograms of bare GCE and the different modified electrodes were performed in stepwise fabrication process of the hNiNS/GQDs/MIPs/GCE with 5.0mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- probe solution at 50mVs-1 from −0.2 to 0.6V. As shown in Fig. 2 A, a pair of typical redox current peaks of the GCE are observed at 0.18V and 0.26V (curve a). When the hNiNS were electrodeposited onto the bare GCE, the peak current of hNiNS/GCE was enhanced (curve b). The peak current observably increased (curve c) after the GQDs depositing onto the hNiNS/GCE, indicating that GQDs as electrode modification play an important role in enhancing the sensor current response. After the modification of washed MIPs layer, an obvious decrease of the redox peaks value was observed (curve d). This can be attributed to that the electron transfer rate and electrical conductivity of the polypyrrole are lower than that of GQDs. Similarly, with the NIP film modification, the redox peaks value decreased compared to hNiNS/GQDs/GCE, too (curve e).

The electrochemical responses towards BPS on different electrodes were investigated in 0.1M CBS containing 5μM BPS. From Fig. 2 B, a small anodic peak was observed for bare GCE, resulting from the irreversible oxidation of phenolic hydroxyl groups of BPS (curve a). After bare GCE was modified with GQDs coated on hNiNS, the peak current of BPS was remarkably enhanced under the same concentration (curve b), indicating the catalysis of hNiNS and GQDs to the oxidation of BPS. While MIPs were deposited on the hNiNS/GQDs/GCE, the peak current of BPS was further enhanced (curve c). This showed that BPS-imprinted MIPs prepared with pyrrole as the functional monomer could recognize BPS molecules through hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking interactions. By contrast, the NIPs without the BPS-imprinted cavities could not promote the response current to BPS (curve d).

The influence of scan rate on the electro-oxidation of BPS at the hNiNS/GQDs/MIPs/GCE was investigated through CVs scanning to obtain information about electrochemical mechanism from the potential relationship between peak currents and corresponding scan rate. CVs were performed with different scan rate from 25 to 300mVs-1 in 5mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- probe solution, according to Fig. S4A. The redox peak currents (I p) varied linearly with the square root of the scan rate, corresponding to the following equation: I p (μA) = 28.5976 v 1/2 (mV1/2 s-1/2) + 73.0362, R 2 = 0.9939 and I p (μA) = − 27.7414 v 1/2 (mV1/2 s-1/2) − 81.6325, R 2 = 0.9911 (Fig. S4B), indicating a diffusion controlled mechanism at the surface of the sensor (Liu et al., 2017c). Afterwards, the active surface area of the modified electrode was calculated based on the Randles – Sevcik equation (Ngamchuea et al., 2014):
(1)
I
p
=
268600
n
3/2
A
D
1
/2
C
v
1/2
where I p is the anodic peak current (A), n is the electron transferred number of the redox event (n = 1), A is active surface area of the modified electrode, D is diffusion coefficient (D = 6.70 × 10-6 cm2 s-1) of 5mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- probe (Liu et al., 2017c), C is concentration of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- (mol·cm-3), and v is scan rate (Vs-1). From the slope of I p vs. v 1/2, the active surface area for hNiNS/GQDs/MIPs/GCE is calculated to be 0.260cm2, which is higher than the geometric area of bare GCE (0.196cm2). Also, a linearly increasing relationship is observed between oxidation peak potential (E p) and the Napierian logarithm of scan rate after scanning CVs in 0.1M CBS (pH 4.0) containing 50μM BPS at different scan rate from Fig. S5A, corresponding to the following equation: E p (V) = 0.0333 lnv (mVs-1) + 0.9174, R 2 = 0.9941 (Fig. S5B). Thus, the value of electron transfer coefficient (α) can be calculated by the slope of the following equation (Liang et al., 2017):
(2)
E
p
=
E
0'
+
R
T
(1-α)nF
ln v
where E 0′is the formal redox potential, T is the temperature of 298K, F is Faraday constant. The electrochemical oxidation of BPS at the modified electrode is a two-electron and two-proton process like that of BPA (Zhu et al., 2014). Therefore, the electron-transfer number (n) of the irreversible oxidation reaction of BPS is 2. Thus, α is calculated to be 0.615.

Furthermore, chronocoulometry was employed to calculate the diffusion coefficient (D) and surface concentration (Г 0) of the analyte. The value of D and Г 0 can be calculated by the slope and intercept of the integrated Cottrell equation (Prasad et al., 2017), respectively:
(3)
Q
= 2nF AC
(
Dt
)

π
-1/2
+
Q
ads
+
Q
dl
(4)
Q
ads
= nF A
Г
0
where the slope of Q vs. t 1/2 is measured to be 2.523 × 10-4 Cs-1/2, C is 50μM, Q dl is double layer charge and Q ads is faradic oxidative charge. For hNiNS/GQDs/MIPs/GCE, Q dl and total charge (Q dl + Q ads) represents the respective intercepts of the chronocoulometry plots in the absence and presence of BPS on the hNiNS /GQDs/MIPs/GCE. The value of D and Г 0 can be calculated to be 7.952 × 10-3 cm-2 s-1 and 2.15 × 10-10 molcm-2 (3.21 × 1013 molecules), respectively.

Also, the standard rate constant (k) at the hNiNS/GQDs/MIPs/GCE is calculated to characterize the electron-transfer kinetics using Velasco equation (Velasco, 1997):
(5)

= 1.11
D
1/2

E
p/2
)
-1/2
v
1/2
where E p/2 is the half peak potential (mV), v is scan rate (50mV/s), the value of k can be calculated to be 9.35 × 10-2 cms-1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of the molar ratio functional monomer to template molecule
ce:section.ce:para: The amount of the cavities formed on the polymer matrix was directly affected by the molar ratio of functional monomer to template molecule. The effect of the molar ratio was shown in the range from 6:1 to 1:1 (Fig. S6A), in which the MIP electrochemical sensor fabricated with a molar ratio of 4:1 exhibited the strongest current response. A stronger current signal indicated the adsorption of more template molecules, which showed that more BPS-imprinted cavities have been formed. Therefore, the optimal molar ratio of functional monomer to template molecule was set as 4:1.

The amount of imprinted BPS cavities also depended on the thickness of the MIP film, which can be easily controlled by adjusting the number of scanning cycles in the process of electropolymerization (Wang et al., 2014). From Fig. S6B, the current response increased with increasing the number of scanning cycles and achieved the maximum current response at 10 cycles. After the scanning cycles were increased to more than 10 cycles, the current response value was decreased rapidly, indicating that the MIP film was too thick to extract the template molecules completely. Hence, 10 cycles was chosen to be the optimal electropolymerization cycle times for suitable thickness with stronger current response.

The DPV current response in Fig. S6C shows that BPS molecules exhibit different electrochemical behaviors when pH ranges from 3.0 to 6.0 with the highest response observed at pH 4.0. When the pH value increased from 3.0 to 4.0, the current response increased gradually. However, the current response decreased rapidly when increasing the pH value from 4.0 to 6.0. This phenomenon suggests that the degree of dissociation of BPS increases when the pH value exceeds 4.0. Thus, CBS with pH value of 4.0 was selected to be the determination solution in the following determinations.

A rapid response is one of the most important parameters evaluating MIECS performance. In this work, the current response of BPS increased with the incubation time reaching up to 90s and no obvious change was observed after 90s from Fig. S6D. Results indicate that 90s of incubation time was enough to achieve the maximum adsorption towards BPS molecules and the MIP film reached adsorption equilibrium time at 90s. Hence, 90s was set as the optimal incubation time for further experiments.

The oxidized products of the bisphenol analogues has been shown to easily attach to the surface of the electrode (Lu et al., 2013a; Zhou et al., 2014a), therefore it is essential to provide enough elution time to clean the oxidized products of BPS. To investigate the influence of elution time on the current response and improve the recognition ability and reproducibility of the MIECS, the MIPs modified-electrode was eluted by methanol-acetic acid (v:v = 9:1) solution for 2–15min, respectively. As shown in Fig. S6E, the current response increased with the elution time varying from 2 to 8min, but the elution achieve equilibrium beyond the elution time of 8min, indicating that 8min was the optimal washing time for the re-adsorption of the MIECS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimal response characteristics of MMIP sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal experimental conditions, DPV was employed to investigate the current response value in 0.1M CBS of pH 4.0 containing different concentration of BPS. Fig. 3 A shows that, as expected, the peak current increases with the increase of BPS concentration. Furthermore, it shows linear dependence between 0.1 to 5μM and 5–50μM concentrations, respectively. The corresponding linear regression equations were I p (μA) = 0.5914 c (μM) + 0.2943, R 2 = 0.9990 and I p (μA) = 0.2175 c (μM) + 2.4692, R 2 = 0.9952 (Fig. 3 B and C) with a low LOD of 0.03μM (S/N = 3, confidence level = 95%). LOD was obtained by the equation (Atar et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2012): LOD = 3 σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of the peak currents of the blank (n = 10) and S represents the slope of the first calibration curve of BPS. The analytic results of other methods comparing with this work for the determination of BPS are listed in the Table 1, showing that the hNiNS/GQDs/MIPs electrochemical sensor provided a wider linear range and a lower limit for BPS detection analysis.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of the imprinted sensor, some structural analogues such as phenol, hydroquinone, p-nitrophenol, and sulfanilic acid were chosen to verify it. The imprinted sensor was immersed in 0.1M CBS containing 5μM and 25μM BPS in the presence of the above-mentioned analogues at equivalent concentration as BPS. According to the anti-interfered experimental results ( Fig. 4 A and B), it was found that these structural analogues had no significant interference for the determination of BPS and the relative standard deviation (RSD) varied from 2.04% to 4.59%, which indicated that the specific adsorption to BPS molecules of the sensor was strong and the MIP film was effective for BPS. In addition, 500-fold inorganic ions such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, NO3 -, SO4 2- and 100-fold amino acids such as L-phenylalanine and L-Serine as much as BPS had no significant influence on the determination of BPS. These results show that the MIECS had good capability to recognize specific target molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and stability of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The reproducibility was evaluated by measuring the current response towards five independently prepared BPS solutions with the same electrode. The calculated RSD is 2.09% at the BPS concentration of 5μM, which indicates that the MIECS has satisfactory reproducibility. The modified GCE was placed in the 4°C refrigerator when not in use. To investigate the stability of the imprinted sensor, the DPV current response was measured after two weeks and the I p value decreased only by 3.3% when compared to the initial current response. This stability indicates that the imprinted electrode can be used for a relatively long time to detect BPS.

The proposed method was applied to detect the concentration of BPS in different brands of mineral water and extraction solutions of some plastic samples. A known amount of BPS standard solution was added to the pretreated sample solutions for performing the recovery experiments. Every sample was detected three times in parallel. To validate the accuracy of the proposed method, the detection results were compared with those obtained by HPLC method. In comparison, the differences between the two methods are acceptable. According to Student T-test, the methods are similar at the confidence level of 95%. The results are listed in the Table S1, showing that the values of recovery vary from 94.4% to 99.6%, which indicates that this imprinted electrode provides a practical and accurate method for the determination of BPS in the sample analysis.

In this paper, we have constructed a highly sensitive and selective molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor for the rapid determination of BPS. The MIECS was fabricated on hNiNS loading GQDs and MIP film modified GCE, respectively. The experimental results show that GQDs coated on hNiNS can promote the electrochemical response of the sensor and MIPs prepared by pyrrole as functional monomer can further enhance the current response towards BPS. Under optimized experimental conditions, BPS in the concentration range from 0.1 to 50μM showed a satisfactory linear relationship on the imprinted electrode with a low limit of detection of 0.03μM (S/N = 3). Additionally, this sensor has been applied successfully to detect BPS in the real sample with excellent selectivity, reproducibility and stability，providing a potential method to monitor BPS and its analogues in the plastic samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant from the Two-way Support Programs of Sichuan Agricultural University (Project No. 03572228), Undergraduate Training Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship (Project No. 201610626087) and the Education Department of Sichuan Provincial, P.R. China (Grant No. 16ZA0039). We thank the anonymous reviewers for their valuable suggestions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.09.016.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: South Afr. J. Chem. Suid Afr. Tydskr. Chem.
